title: Pleural Tap & Chest Drain
oneLiner: Two procedures to diagnose and manage pleural effusions/pneumothorax; correct technique and complication recognition prevent catastrophic harm.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension pneumothorax** — *single finding*: tracheal deviation away from side of decreased air entry + haemodynamic instability (hypotension, distended neck veins). MUST be decompressed immediately with large-bore cannula (2nd ICS mid-clavicular line) before any imaging.
- **Massive haemothorax** — *single finding*: >1500 mL blood on chest drain insertion OR ongoing bleeding >200 mL/hour for 2-4 hours. Immediate large-bore drain + crossmatch + senior surgical review.
- **Iatrogenic intercostal artery laceration** — *single finding*: rapid cardiovascular collapse during/after drain insertion, especially in elderly or patients with coagulopathy. Needle/catheter is still in situ — do not remove; apply direct pressure and call for emergency thoracotomy.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** progressive dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, reduced exercise tolerance, cough (may be non-productive or purulent if empyema)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - *Effusion:* stony dullness to percussion, reduced breath sounds, decreased vocal resonance, possible tracheal shift away from effusion (large)
  - *Pneumothorax:* hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, tracheal shift away (tension), Hammon’s crunch (small)
  - *Empyema:* fevers, rigours, foul-smelling sputum, weight loss
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Exudative effusion (most common)** → unilateral, protein >30 g/L, LDH >200 IU/L, often parapneumonic/malignant  
- **Transudative effusion** → bilateral, protein <30 g/L, due to heart failure, cirrhosis, renal failure  
- **Empyema** → putrid odour, pH <7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, frank pus on aspiration  
- **Haemothorax** → bloody aspirate, haematocrit >50% of peripheral blood  
- **Chylothorax** → milky-white fluid, triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L  
- **Pneumothorax (spontaneous/secondary)** → no fluid level on CXR, sharp chest pain, hyperresonance  
- **Splitter (at bedside):** “Aspirate appearance + pH” — straw-coloured, pH >7.4 → transudate; cloudy/turbid, pH <7.2 → empyema; bloody → exclude haemothorax
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Ultrasound (if trained): confirm fluid depth, locate safe puncture site (rib space, away from diaphragm/lung), guide needle entry  
  - Pleural aspiration: 50-60 mL syringe, 21G needle, send for pH, protein, LDH, glucose, microbiology (culture + Gram stain), cytology (if suspected malignancy)  
  - Check clotting (INR <1.5, platelets >50 x10^9/L) and full consent  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U+E, LFT, CRP, coagulation screen, blood cultures (if fever)  
  - Consider NT-proBNP (if suspected heart failure)  
  - LDH and total protein (Light’s criteria)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Chest X-ray (PA + lateral)** — detect effusion >200 mL, assess pneumothorax, note air-fluid levels  
  - Second-line: **CT chest with contrast** — complex loculated effusion, empyema, suspected malignancy, guide drain placement  
  - Ultrasound: gold standard for locating diaphragmatic border and fluid depth  
- **Never-miss test:** **Check INR and platelet count before any pleural procedure** — if abnormal, correct (vitamin K, FFP, platelets per trust policy). Omission can cause fatal bleeding.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - For **tension pneumothorax**: immediate needle decompression (14G cannula, 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line) → then chest drain insertion  
  - For **pleural effusion (diagnostic tap):** landmark (safe triangle — mid-axillary line, 5th-6th ICS, above the rib to avoid neurovascular bundle), aseptic technique, local anaesthetic (1% lidocaine), aspirate ± send samples  
  - For **drain insertion (therapeutic):** ultrasound if available, wire-guided (Seldinger) technique preferred. Use 12-14Fr for simple effusion, 20-28Fr for empyema/haemothorax. Attach to underwater seal drainage system (3-chamber or commercial kit).  
  - Secure drain with stay suture, occlusive dressing, connect to drainage bag (gravity or low wall suction –20 cmH₂O if needed per BTS guidance).  
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**  
  - Document drain output (colour, volume, any air leak) every 4 hours or more frequently if high output.  
  - Position patient semi-recumbent to promote drainage.  
  - Keep drainage system below chest level to prevent reflux.  
  - If suction used, monitor for pain, air leak, or re-expansion pulmonary oedema.  
  - Daily CXR to confirm lung expansion and check drain position.  
  - Empiric antibiotics if empyema (e.g., co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO/IV — [check local policy] for severe infection) pending culture results.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Manage underlying cause (heart failure, malignancy, infection).  
  - Remove drain once output <150 mL/day and lung expanded on CXR. Usually wait 24 h after drain removal to ensure no re-accumulation.  
  - Follow-up chest X-ray at 2-4 weeks.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: maintain IV access, consider 1L NS/24h if nil by mouth (NBM) for procedure — **check local fluid policy**.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: assess bleeding risk — if drain inserted for haemothorax, may need modified prophylaxis; otherwise standard LWMH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) once haemostasis secured.  
  - Diet: nothing by mouth until after procedure (local anaesthetic safe, but if sedation used, NBM 4h).  
  - Observation frequency: standard NEWS2 (at least 4-hourly), plus drain output chart, pain assessment.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL).  
  - Dose: infiltrate skin (0.5–1 mL), then deeper tissues (up to 20 mL total for therapeutic tap). Max dose 3 mg/kg (e.g., 210 mg for 70 kg adult = 21 mL of 1%). Never exceed without checking.  
  - Contraindications: severe allergy to amide anaesthetics (rare).  
  - Interaction: caution with hepatic impairment (reduced metabolism).  
- **Prophylactic antibiotics:** Not routinely indicated for simple chest drains. For traumatic haemothorax or empyema, consider co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam (**[check local policy]**).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lidocaine overdose** (seizure, cardiac arrest) — always aspirate before injecting to avoid intravascular injection. Maximum total dose must be calculated and documented.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Pneumothorax (caused by lung puncture) — may require second drain  
  - Haemorrhage (intercostal artery injury, especially in elderly/rib fractures) — apply pressure, urgent vascular surgery  
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (rapid drainage >1.5L in one go) — slow drainage, supportive care  
  - Empyema (introduction of infection) — strict asepsis  
  - Vagal reaction (syncope during procedure) — lie patient flat, IV atropine if needed  
  - Catheter misplacement (subcutaneous, intraparenchymal, intra-abdominal) — stop, get CXR/CT  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Loculated effusion (incomplete drainage) — intrapleural fibrinolytics (e.g., alteplase + DNase — **[check local policy]**) or surgical decortication  
  - Drain site infection / cellulitis — microbiological swab, antibiotics  
  - Tube blockage (clot/fibrin) — gentle flushing with normal saline (if output ceases)  
  - Persistent air leak (bronchopleural fistula) — consider chest drain suction, surgical repair
sections.Prognosis:
- Simple effusion (transudative): excellent with treatment of cause.  
- Parapneumonic effusion: mortality 5-15% if develops empyema.  
- Malignant effusion: median survival 4-6 months. Chest drain (talc pleurodesis) can improve symptoms.  
- Pneumothorax: recurrence 30-50% in spontaneous cases; supportive care or surgery (pleurectomy) may reduce recurrence.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform ultrasound** (if available) to mark the fluid pocket and avoid diaphragm/liver/spleen.  
- **Always insert drain “above the rib”** — the neurovascular bundle runs under each rib (posteriorly). Needle direction: just above the rib margin.  
- **Clamp the drain briefly** (maximum 60 seconds) when disconnecting tubing or changing drainage bag to prevent air entry. Never clamp continuously in pneumothorax (risk of tension).  
- **Use a 3-chamber drainage system** (collection chamber → water seal (for bubbling) → suction control). Confirm water seal at 2 cm H₂O level.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never insert a drain without checking coagulation status** — risk of life-threatening haemorrhage.  
- **Never insert a drain through the ‘safe triangle’ if the effusion is not in that location** — always confirm with ultrasound or CT.  
- **Never remove a chest drain without a confirming CXR** that shows lung expanded and no re-accumulation.  
- **Never give prophylactic antibiotics routinely** — not indicated; only for suspected empyema or traumatic haemothorax per local policy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening shortness of breath  
  - Uncontrolled chest pain  
  - Fever or chills (suggests infection)  
  - Drain site becomes red/swollen/pus discharge  
  - Sudden increase in drain output (e.g., >200 mL in one hour)  
- **Follow-up:** Chest X-ray in 2-4 weeks; clinic review with respiratory team within 1 week of drain removal.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising persistently  
  - Signs of tension despite drain (tracheal deviation, hypotension) — check drain patency and consider repeat decompression  
  - Massive haemorrhage >1500 mL immediate output or ongoing at >200 mL/hour  
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (sudden dyspnoea, hypoxia, frothy sputum)  
  - Drain blockage (no output with persistent pneumothorax) — urgent guidance for flushing or replacement  
  - GCS <15 or seizure during procedure (lidocaine toxicity)

rawMarkdown:
## Pleural Tap & Chest Drain

**One-liner:** Two procedures to diagnose and manage pleural effusions/pneumothorax; correct technique and complication recognition prevent catastrophic harm.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension pneumothorax** — *single finding*: tracheal deviation away from side of decreased air entry + haemodynamic instability (hypotension, distended neck veins). MUST be decompressed immediately with large-bore cannula (2nd ICS mid-clavicular line) before any imaging.
- **Massive haemothorax** — *single finding*: >1500 mL blood on chest drain insertion OR ongoing bleeding >200 mL/hour for 2-4 hours. Immediate large-bore drain + crossmatch + senior surgical review.
- **Iatrogenic intercostal artery laceration** — *single finding*: rapid cardiovascular collapse during/after drain insertion, especially in elderly or patients with coagulopathy. Needle/catheter is still in situ — do not remove; apply direct pressure and call for emergency thoracotomy.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** progressive dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, reduced exercise tolerance, cough (may be non-productive or purulent if empyema)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - *Effusion:* stony dullness to percussion, reduced breath sounds, decreased vocal resonance, possible tracheal shift away from effusion (large)
  - *Pneumothorax:* hyperresonance, absent breath sounds, tracheal shift away (tension), Hammon’s crunch (small)
  - *Empyema:* fevers, rigours, foul-smelling sputum, weight loss

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Exudative effusion (most common)** → unilateral, protein >30 g/L, LDH >200 IU/L, often parapneumonic/malignant  
- **Transudative effusion** → bilateral, protein <30 g/L, due to heart failure, cirrhosis, renal failure  
- **Empyema** → putrid odour, pH <7.2, glucose <2.2 mmol/L, frank pus on aspiration  
- **Haemothorax** → bloody aspirate, haematocrit >50% of peripheral blood  
- **Chylothorax** → milky-white fluid, triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L  
- **Pneumothorax (spontaneous/secondary)** → no fluid level on CXR, sharp chest pain, hyperresonance  
- **Splitter (at bedside):** “Aspirate appearance + pH” — straw-coloured, pH >7.4 → transudate; cloudy/turbid, pH <7.2 → empyema; bloody → exclude haemothorax

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Ultrasound (if trained): confirm fluid depth, locate safe puncture site (rib space, away from diaphragm/lung), guide needle entry  
  - Pleural aspiration: 50-60 mL syringe, 21G needle, send for pH, protein, LDH, glucose, microbiology (culture + Gram stain), cytology (if suspected malignancy)  
  - Check clotting (INR <1.5, platelets >50 x10^9/L) and full consent  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U+E, LFT, CRP, coagulation screen, blood cultures (if fever)  
  - Consider NT-proBNP (if suspected heart failure)  
  - LDH and total protein (Light’s criteria)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: **Chest X-ray (PA + lateral)** — detect effusion >200 mL, assess pneumothorax, note air-fluid levels  
  - Second-line: **CT chest with contrast** — complex loculated effusion, empyema, suspected malignancy, guide drain placement  
  - Ultrasound: gold standard for locating diaphragmatic border and fluid depth  
- **Never-miss test:** **Check INR and platelet count before any pleural procedure** — if abnormal, correct (vitamin K, FFP, platelets per trust policy). Omission can cause fatal bleeding.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - For **tension pneumothorax**: immediate needle decompression (14G cannula, 2nd ICS mid-clavicular line) → then chest drain insertion  
  - For **pleural effusion (diagnostic tap):** landmark (safe triangle — mid-axillary line, 5th-6th ICS, above the rib to avoid neurovascular bundle), aseptic technique, local anaesthetic (1% lidocaine), aspirate ± send samples  
  - For **drain insertion (therapeutic):** ultrasound if available, wire-guided (Seldinger) technique preferred. Use 12-14Fr for simple effusion, 20-28Fr for empyema/haemothorax. Attach to underwater seal drainage system (3-chamber or commercial kit).  
  - Secure drain with stay suture, occlusive dressing, connect to drainage bag (gravity or low wall suction –20 cmH₂O if needed per BTS guidance).  
- **Ward (first 24-72 h):**  
  - Document drain output (colour, volume, any air leak) every 4 hours or more frequently if high output.  
  - Position patient semi-recumbent to promote drainage.  
  - Keep drainage system below chest level to prevent reflux.  
  - If suction used, monitor for pain, air leak, or re-expansion pulmonary oedema.  
  - Daily CXR to confirm lung expansion and check drain position.  
  - Empiric antibiotics if empyema (e.g., co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO/IV — [check local policy] for severe infection) pending culture results.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Manage underlying cause (heart failure, malignancy, infection).  
  - Remove drain once output <150 mL/day and lung expanded on CXR. Usually wait 24 h after drain removal to ensure no re-accumulation.  
  - Follow-up chest X-ray at 2-4 weeks.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: maintain IV access, consider 1L NS/24h if nil by mouth (NBM) for procedure — **check local fluid policy**.  
  - VTE prophylaxis: assess bleeding risk — if drain inserted for haemothorax, may need modified prophylaxis; otherwise standard LWMH (e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) once haemostasis secured.  
  - Diet: nothing by mouth until after procedure (local anaesthetic safe, but if sedation used, NBM 4h).  
  - Observation frequency: standard NEWS2 (at least 4-hourly), plus drain output chart, pain assessment.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL).  
  - Dose: infiltrate skin (0.5–1 mL), then deeper tissues (up to 20 mL total for therapeutic tap). Max dose 3 mg/kg (e.g., 210 mg for 70 kg adult = 21 mL of 1%). Never exceed without checking.  
  - Contraindications: severe allergy to amide anaesthetics (rare).  
  - Interaction: caution with hepatic impairment (reduced metabolism).  
- **Prophylactic antibiotics:** Not routinely indicated for simple chest drains. For traumatic haemothorax or empyema, consider co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam (**[check local policy]**).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Lidocaine overdose** (seizure, cardiac arrest) — always aspirate before injecting to avoid intravascular injection. Maximum total dose must be calculated and documented.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Pneumothorax (caused by lung puncture) — may require second drain  
  - Haemorrhage (intercostal artery injury, especially in elderly/rib fractures) — apply pressure, urgent vascular surgery  
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (rapid drainage >1.5L in one go) — slow drainage, supportive care  
  - Empyema (introduction of infection) — strict asepsis  
  - Vagal reaction (syncope during procedure) — lie patient flat, IV atropine if needed  
  - Catheter misplacement (subcutaneous, intraparenchymal, intra-abdominal) — stop, get CXR/CT  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Loculated effusion (incomplete drainage) — intrapleural fibrinolytics (e.g., alteplase + DNase — **[check local policy]**) or surgical decortication  
  - Drain site infection / cellulitis — microbiological swab, antibiotics  
  - Tube blockage (clot/fibrin) — gentle flushing with normal saline (if output ceases)  
  - Persistent air leak (bronchopleural fistula) — consider chest drain suction, surgical repair

### Prognosis
- Simple effusion (transudative): excellent with treatment of cause.  
- Parapneumonic effusion: mortality 5-15% if develops empyema.  
- Malignant effusion: median survival 4-6 months. Chest drain (talc pleurodesis) can improve symptoms.  
- Pneumothorax: recurrence 30-50% in spontaneous cases; supportive care or surgery (pleurectomy) may reduce recurrence.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform ultrasound** (if available) to mark the fluid pocket and avoid diaphragm/liver/spleen.  
- **Always insert drain “above the rib”** — the neurovascular bundle runs under each rib (posteriorly). Needle direction: just above the rib margin.  
- **Clamp the drain briefly** (maximum 60 seconds) when disconnecting tubing or changing drainage bag to prevent air entry. Never clamp continuously in pneumothorax (risk of tension).  
- **Use a 3-chamber drainage system** (collection chamber → water seal (for bubbling) → suction control). Confirm water seal at 2 cm H₂O level.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never insert a drain without checking coagulation status** — risk of life-threatening haemorrhage.  
- **Never insert a drain through the ‘safe triangle’ if the effusion is not in that location** — always confirm with ultrasound or CT.  
- **Never remove a chest drain without a confirming CXR** that shows lung expanded and no re-accumulation.  
- **Never give prophylactic antibiotics routinely** — not indicated; only for suspected empyema or traumatic haemothorax per local policy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening shortness of breath  
  - Uncontrolled chest pain  
  - Fever or chills (suggests infection)  
  - Drain site becomes red/swollen/pus discharge  
  - Sudden increase in drain output (e.g., >200 mL in one hour)  
- **Follow-up:** Chest X-ray in 2-4 weeks; clinic review with respiratory team within 1 week of drain removal.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising persistently  
  - Signs of tension despite drain (tracheal deviation, hypotension) — check drain patency and consider repeat decompression  
  - Massive haemorrhage >1500 mL immediate output or ongoing at >200 mL/hour  
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (sudden dyspnoea, hypoxia, frothy sputum)  
  - Drain blockage (no output with persistent pneumothorax) — urgent guidance for flushing or replacement  
  - GCS <15 or seizure during procedure (lidocaine toxicity)
